title: Te Hauora o te Awa: The Health of Our River / Te Hauora o te Awa: Te Oranga o tō tātou Awa
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway flowing through our rohe. Students will explore how Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors traditionally monitored and maintained the health of this awa as an indicator of community wellbeing, using practices like checking water clarity, observing aquatic life, and gathering medicinal plants from its banks.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three traditional Ngāti Kahungunu practices for monitoring awa health and explain their connection to community wellbeing.
Conduct a basic water quality assessment of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using both mātauranga Māori indicators (e.g., presence of tuna) and scientific measures (e.g., clarity tube).
Create a personal 'Hauora Awa' plan that outlines one action to contribute to the health of the local waterway and explain how this action supports their own holistic health (hauora).

activities.title: He Kōrero mō te Awa: Story of the River
activities.description: Students gather at the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. A local kaumātua shares pūrākau (traditional stories) about the awa, explaining its significance as a source of food, transport, healing, and spiritual connection for Ngāti Kahungunu. Students create a collaborative sketch map of the awa, annotating it with these cultural and historical points of significance.

activities.title: Nga Tohu Hauora o te Awa: Signs of the River's Health
activities.description: In small groups, students rotate through stations to learn traditional and modern methods of assessing the health of the awa. Stations include: using a homemade clarity tube (wai māori); identifying riparian plants used in rongoā (traditional medicine); looking for signs of tuna (eels) and kōura (freshwater crayfish); and discussing how the river's condition reflects the hauora of the surrounding community.

activities.title: Mahi Tiaki: Kaitiakitanga in Action
activities.description: Guided by a representative from a local environmental group (e.g., Nga Whenua Rahui), students participate in a hands-on kaitiaki activity. This could involve planting native sedges on the riverbank to prevent erosion, building and installing a simple sediment trap, or conducting a litter clean-up. The focus is on the physical action of caring for the environment and how this active guardianship contributes to their own sense of wellbeing and identity.

activities.title: Te Whare Tapa Whā o te Awa: The Four-Sided House of the River
activities.description: Back in the classroom, students work in groups to analyse the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku through the lens of Te Whare Tapa Whā. They create a poster or digital presentation exploring: Taha Tinana (Physical Health - water quality, biodiversity); Taha Hinengaro (Mental & Emotional Health - the awa as a place for reflection and calm); Taha Whānau (Social Health - the awa as a gathering place); and Taha Wairua (Spiritual Health - the mauri/life force of the awa).

resources: Local Kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu (e.g., Aunty Hine Smith) to share pūrākau
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'Wai Wise' education resources and water testing kits
The book 'Rongoā Māori: A Practical Guide to Traditional Māori Medicine' by Rob McGowan
Representative from 'Nga Whenua Rahui' or a similar local kaitiaki environmental group

tasks: Group presentation of their 'Te Whare Tapa Whā o te Awa' analysis, demonstrating understanding of the interconnection between environmental and personal health.
Submission of their personal 'Hauora Awa' plan, clearly outlining a specific, actionable commitment to the awa and explaining its benefit to their wellbeing.

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: Strand A - Personal Health and Physical Development (A3 - Safety Management; A4 - Personal Identity); Strand C - Relationships with Other People (C2 - Identity, Sensitivity, and Respect); Strand D - Healthy Communities and Environments (D1 - Societal Attitudes and Values; D3 - Community Resources)
Science: Nature of Science - Participating and Contributing; Living World - Ecology
